summary: Gruber recounts seeing a young boy with two artificial legs running happily at Disney World, marveling at the technology that made it possible. He notes that the prosthetics were so advanced the boy could run with only a slight limp. Gruber reflects that this technology simply didn't exist a generation ago. He uses the moment as a counterpoint to the despair that comes from focusing solely on current events, arguing that science and progress steadily make the world better. The post serves as an optimistic New Year's message.
key_points: A young boy with bilateral prosthetic legs was running freely at Disney World, nearly indistinguishable from other children
The prosthetic technology enabling this didn't exist one generation ago, illustrating the pace of progress
Focusing only on current events makes it easy to despair, but the broader arc of science bends toward improvement
The post uses a single vivid observation to make a larger philosophical point about human progress
money_quotes: If he had been wearing pants instead of shorts, you'd have simply thought he had a bit of a limp.

He was born without legs, but yet there he was, galloping across the playground on a warm, sunny December morning, every bit as happy, excited, and carefree as every little kid in the world ought to be.
His legs — sleek, lightweight, and impressively dextrous — were inspiring and beautiful.
Focus solely on current events and it's all too easy to despair at the state of the world.
But science and progress march ever forward, and the world is a better place today than it used to be.
key_insight: A single moment of a child running on prosthetic legs that didn't exist a generation ago is a powerful reminder that science and progress quietly make the world better, even when current events suggest otherwise.
